n: Church of St. Mary of the Mongols
hook: The only Byzantine church in the city never turned into a mosque
facts: Rebuilt around 1281 by Maria Palaiologina, illegitimate daughter of Emperor Michael VIII and widow of a Mongol khan
Held by the Greek Orthodox community continuously thanks to a decree of Mehmed II after the 1453 conquest
Built of red brick, earning the Turkish nickname Kanli Kilise, the Bloody Church
Usually locked; entry by ringing the caretaker on the steep lane below
legend: Locals say the red name comes from Orthodox blood that ran down the slope into the Golden Horn during the conquest
Rumour claims a secret tunnel links the church to Hagia Sophia
w: Maria Palaiologina, a Byzantine princess married off to a Mongol khan, came home widowed around 1281 and rebuilt this red-brick church. A decree from Mehmed II spared it after 1453, so it alone among the city's Byzantine churches never became a mosque. Locals call it Kanli Kilise, the Bloody Church. Still ringing for a caretaker.

hook: Sea-wall ruins of the lost imperial palace, named for a stone lion and bull
facts: Marble facade and three great windows survive in the Marmara sea wall east of Kucuk Ayasofya
Imperial summer residence expanded under Justinian and dramatically enlarged by Theophilos in the 9th century
Name comes from a statue group of a lion mauling a bull that stood at its private harbour
Long fenced off and overgrown; recent IBB excavations aim to open it as an open-air site
w: Three vast marble windows hang in the Marmara sea wall, all that survives of an imperial palace where emperors received envoys and slipped out to a private harbour. The name remembers a vanished statue of a lion savaging a bull. Trains rattle past below the rubble. Byzantium's grandeur, left to the weeds.

hook: The 1876 boza parlour where they keep Ataturk's drinking glass under glass
facts: Opened in 1876 by Haci Sadik Bey, an Albanian from Prizren, in the same Vefa premises ever since
Serves boza, a thick fermented millet drink, topped with cinnamon and roasted chickpeas
Ferments in marble vats rather than wooden barrels, the house's claim to a cleaner taste
Still run by descendants of the founder, nearly 150 years on
legend: The shop keeps a glass said to be the one Ataturk drank from on a 1937 visit
w: An Albanian from Prizren opened this tiled parlour in 1876 to sell a smoother boza, the thick fermented-millet drink dusted with cinnamon and roasted chickpeas. They ferment it in marble vats, not barrels. A glass kept under a bell jar supposedly held Ataturk's boza in 1937. Winter in a tumbler, six generations deep.

hook: Curving Art Nouveau staircase built so the banker's grandsons couldn't fall far
facts: Built around 1870-1880 by Abraham Salomon Camondo, the Ottoman Empire's leading Jewish banker
Fuses Neo-Baroque and early Art Nouveau in a hexagonal switchback on Bankalar Caddesi
Curves are widely said to slow a slipping child's fall rather than drop them straight down
The Camondo family's last descendants were murdered at Auschwitz in 1943-1945
w: Around 1870 the Ottoman Empire's richest Jewish banker built these stairs as a shortcut from his house to Banks Street, and for grandsons walking to school. The hexagonal curves, half Baroque and half Art Nouveau, supposedly break a child's fall instead of letting it plunge. The family later died at Auschwitz. Tenderness in stone.

hook: The oldest Roman monument still standing in the city, hiding in a park
facts: Roman victory column of the 3rd or 4th century, the oldest intact Roman monument in Istanbul
Latin base reads FORTVNAE REDVCI OB DEVICTOS GOTHOS, to Fortune returning after the Goths were beaten
Visible inscription replaced an earlier one whose text is lost
Once topped by a bust of Tyche, removed by later Christians
legend: A vanished carving on the base is reported to have read IC XC NIKA, Jesus Christ conquers
w: Tourists stream past it to the Topkapi gate, never noticing the oldest Roman thing still upright in town. The base brags in Latin about beating the Goths, over an older inscription nobody can read. A statue of Fortune once crowned it before Christians pulled her down. Eighteen metres of marble, quietly outlasting everyone.

hook: Byzantium's imperial prison, where four emperors were locked in their own walls
facts: Substructures of the Blachernae Palace built into the land walls above the Golden Horn
Named for the general Michael Anemas, jailed after a failed plot against Alexios I around 1107
Four Byzantine emperors were later imprisoned here, plus a son of Sultan Murad I
A vaulted cistern survives inside the surviving tower
w: Where the great land walls drop toward the Golden Horn, dank vaulted cells burrow into the Blachernae Palace. They carry the name of a general jailed for plotting against his emperor around 1107, but four emperors of Byzantium also rotted in here, deposed and blinded. A cistern still pools in the dark. Power's waiting room.

hook: A novelist's love story built as a real museum, full of one heroine's objects
facts: Created by Nobel laureate Orhan Pamuk in a 19th-century wooden house as a companion to his novel
Opened in 2012 with vitrines keyed to the book's chapters
One wall mounts 4,213 cigarette butts the fictional Fusun is said to have smoked
The novel doubles as an entry ticket; Pamuk won a European museum prize for it
w: Orhan Pamuk wrote a novel about an obsessive love, then built the museum it describes inside a Cukurcuma townhouse, one vitrine per chapter. A whole wall holds 4,213 cigarette butts the heroine supposedly smoked. The book itself works as your ticket. Fiction and fetish, catalogued in glass cases.

hook: The city's oldest whirling-dervish lodge, with a silent house of the dead
facts: Founded in 1491 on the site of a Byzantine monastery, the first Mevlevi lodge in Istanbul
Octagonal semahane hall still hosts sema, the dervishes' turning ceremony
Garden cemetery called Hamusan, the silent ones, holds Ottoman poets and notables
Buried here is Ibrahim Muteferrika, who set up the first Ottoman Turkish printing press
w: Tucked off Istiklal on a Byzantine monastery's bones, this 1491 lodge gave the city its first whirling dervishes, and the octagonal hall still turns them on Sunday nights. Out back lies the Hamusan, the house of the silent, where Ottoman poets sleep beside Muteferrika, who printed the empire's first Turkish books. Spinning by the dead.

hook: A Sinan mosque embedded with four fragments of the Kaaba's Black Stone
facts: Built by Mimar Sinan in 1571-72 for grand vizier Sokollu Mehmed Pasha and his wife Ismihan Sultan
Holds four pieces of the sacred Black Stone of Mecca, set over the door, mihrab and minbar
Even Suleyman the Magnificent received only one such fragment, a mark of the patron's standing
Interior sheathed in Iznik tiles from the peak period of Ottoman ceramics
w: Sinan tucked this jewel into a Kadirga slope in 1571, its walls a jungle of crimson and green Iznik tulips. Set into the doorway, mihrab and pulpit sit four fragments of the Kaaba's Black Stone; Suleyman the Magnificent rated only one. The most powerful man in the empire, showing off in quarried holiness. Often empty.

hook: A crumbling 17th-century caravanserai with a hidden rooftop over the city
facts: The largest historic han in Istanbul, founded in 1651 by Kosem Sultan, mother of two sultans
Vast square courtyard 55 metres a side, once lodging for foreign merchants and their goods
Now a warren of textile and craft workshops, decayed and labyrinthine
A small Shia Iranian mescit survives within the complex
w: Kosem Sultan, the most powerful woman in Ottoman history, raised the city's biggest caravanserai in 1651 to bankroll her schemes. Three centuries on it sags into a maze of workshops, looms clattering behind peeling arches, a Persian prayer room hidden inside. Climb past the textile men and the whole skyline opens up. Decay with a view.

hook: A century-old meatball counter that refuses to put spices in anything
facts: Opened in 1920 by Mehmet Seracettin Efendi beside the Sultanahmet tram stop
Grilled kofte made from beef, bread, salt and a little onion, no spices at all
Started under the patriotic name Turan Koftecisi in the new republic's first years
Still trading from the same spot more than a century later
w: Since 1920 this plain counter on Divan Yolu has grilled the same stubborn meatball: beef, bread, salt, a whisper of onion, and absolutely no spice. The founder migrated west and named his stall after a patriotic dream of Turan. A hundred years on, it still says no to everyone wanting it fancier. Defiantly, gloriously bare.

hook: The barracks tower where the Lady with the Lamp ran her Crimean wards
facts: Inside the northwest tower of the colossal Selimiye Barracks on the Asian shore
British army hospital from 1854-56, where Nightingale nursed Crimean War wounded
Her rooms became a museum in 1954 with her desk, lamp and a bracelet from Sultan Abdulmecid
Still an active military base; entry needs ID and advance permission
w: In a corner tower of a still-working Ottoman barracks across the water, a young Englishwoman dragged Victorian battlefield medicine into the modern age. Her desk, lamp and a bracelet gifted by the sultan sit where she worked through the Crimean dead. Soldiers guard the door; you book ahead with your passport. The lamp, kept lit.
